past_medical_history: BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma diagnosed 14 months ago (lungs, breast, liver, bone)
Left temporal lobe brain metastasis (0.5 cm) identified on surveillance MRI 4 months ago
Currently receiving ipilimumab plus nivolumab immunotherapy (started 2 cycles ago)
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 25
Mild iron deficiency anemia corrected 2 years ago
family_history: Mother with breast cancer diagnosed at age 58, currently in remission
Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis diagnosed at age 35
Paternal grandfather had seizures in late life attributed to a stroke
No known family history of epilepsy in younger relatives

social_history.occupation: Graphic designer, currently on disability leave due to metastatic melanoma treatment
social_history.alcohol: Previously social drinker, abstinent since starting immunotherapy
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current or past illicit drug use
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Limited due to treatment fatigue, walks daily when able

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Mildly fatigued in appearance. Speech fluent without paraphasic errors. Naming intact. Repetition and comprehension normal. Attention intact on digit span (6 forward, 4 backward). Memory 3/3 registration, 2/3 recall at 5 minutes (recalled third word with category cue). Clock drawing normal.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild subjective left facial heaviness noted by patient but no objective asymmetry on examination. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk. Tone mildly increased in bilateral lower extremities. Strength: upper extremities 5/5 bilaterally; right hip flexion 4+/5, left hip flexion 5/5; knee extension 5/5 bilaterally; ankle dorsiflexion 5-/5 on right, 5/5 on left. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense normal at ankles bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ on left, 1+ on right. Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Right plantar response equivocal, left plantar flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly clumsy on right, normal on left. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Slightly wide-based, mild right foot drag noted on extended walking. Tandem gait mildly unsteady. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a brief episode lasting approximately 20 seconds in which she stopped speaking, appeared to stare blankly to the right, and made repetitive lip-smacking movements. She then resumed conversation but was briefly confused about what had been discussed. She attributed this to fatigue from chemotherapy. A second similar episode of 15 seconds occurred 40 minutes later with an associated hand automatism (picking at the bedsheets with her right hand). She denied awareness of either episode.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive weakness in the legs and recurrent episodes of unresponsiveness attributed to treatment side effects
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Harmon is a 30-year-old right-handed woman with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma currently receiving combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab who is admitted for evaluation of progressive neurological symptoms. Approximately ten days after her second immunotherapy cycle, she began noticing diffuse fatigue and mild weakness predominantly in her lower extremities, which she and her oncology team initially attributed to treatment-related toxicity. Over the following week, she developed intermittent episodes of unresponsiveness that were first noticed by her partner. During these episodes, she would stop mid-conversation, stare blankly for 15 to 30 seconds, and occasionally make repetitive chewing or lip-smacking movements. She has no recollection of these events and has been dismissing her partner's concerns as exaggeration of ordinary cancer-related fatigue and inattention.

In the past five days, the episodes have become more frequent, now occurring three to five times per day. On two occasions her partner observed her suddenly pause while eating, turn her head slowly to the right, and make fumbling movements with her right hand, followed by approximately one minute of confusion during which she was unable to answer simple questions. She has also described a peculiar sensation that precedes some of the episodes: a rising warmth from her abdomen accompanied by an intense feeling of deja vu and an unusual metallic taste. She initially attributed these sensations to nausea from her immunotherapy but acknowledges that they are distinctly different from the chemotherapy-related nausea she experienced previously.

Her oncology team is primarily concerned about immune-mediated neurotoxicity, specifically an immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated neuropathy or encephalitis, given the temporal relationship between her second immunotherapy cycle and symptom onset. There is also concern about progression of her known left temporal brain metastasis. The mild lower extremity weakness adds complexity, as it could represent a concurrent immune-mediated peripheral neuropathy (such as Guillain-Barre syndrome, which has been reported with checkpoint inhibitors), spinal metastatic disease causing early cord compression, or simply deconditioning. She was started on prophylactic levetiracetam when the brain metastasis was first discovered, but adherence has been inconsistent due to side effects of irritability and fatigue. She reports sometimes skipping her evening dose. An outpatient routine EEG performed at the oncology clinic two weeks ago was reportedly normal. She is now admitted to the neurology service for comprehensive evaluation of the paroxysmal episodes and progressive weakness.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, mildly low in amplitude but reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Mild amplitude asymmetry with lower amplitude over the left temporal region
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves present; brief stage II sleep with sleep spindles

findings.frequency: Intermittent bursts of 4-5 Hz theta activity, occurring every 2-3 minutes
findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta waves, monomorphic, moderate amplitude
Increased beta activity and sharper morphology of normal background rhythms over left temporal area, likely related to skull or soft tissue changes

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions, left greater than right
findings.morphology: Arciform, 6-11 Hz, without aftergoing slow wave

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, partially obscures temporal regions during wakefulness
Intermittent high impedance causing artifact that mimics sharp transients

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. Mild diffuse background slowing with slight asymmetry (more prominent left temporal slowing). Returned to baseline within 90 seconds. No definite epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal symmetric photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG due to intermittent left temporal slowing. This is a non-specific finding that may correlate with the known left temporal structural lesion. Breach rhythm over the left temporal region complicates interpretation of sharp waveforms. Wicket spikes identified as benign variant. No definite epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute recording. Note: A single routine EEG has limited sensitivity (~29-55%) for detecting interictal epileptiform discharges. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Intermittent muscle artifact and electrode impedance issues at T3 reduce confidence in interpretation of left temporal sharp transients. No clinical events occurred during the recording.

findings.location: Left temporal lobe, mesial and lateral

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with surrounding edema extending to left hippocampal formation
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Homogeneous ring enhancement with central non-enhancing component

findings.mass_effect: Mild mass effect on the left temporal horn with partial effacement. No midline shift.

findings.location: Left temporal lobe extending into the left hippocampal formation and parahippocampal gyrus
findings.size: Extends approximately 2 cm beyond enhancing lesion margins

findings.location: Multiple thoracic and lumbar vertebral bodies (T4, T7, T11, L2, L4)
findings.size: Variable, largest 1.2 cm in T7 vertebral body

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense replacing normal marrow fat signal

findings.mass_effect: No epidural extension. No spinal cord compression. No spinal canal narrowing.

additional_observations: Left temporal metastasis has significantly increased in size compared to prior imaging (0.5 cm to 1.6 cm over 4 months)
Perilesional edema involves the left hippocampal formation, which may contribute to seizure susceptibility
Spinal vertebral metastases are widespread but without cord compression or epidural disease
No evidence of hemorrhage within the brain metastasis
mri.impression: Interval growth of the known left temporal lobe metastasis (now 1.6 x 1.4 cm, previously 0.5 cm). Significant perilesional edema extending into the left hippocampal formation and mesial temporal structures. Widespread vertebral metastases without cord compression. No new intracranial lesions. The temporal lesion with hippocampal edema involvement may serve as an epileptogenic focus. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Non-specific T-wave flattening in leads III and aVF
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with borderline rate. Non-specific T-wave changes in inferior leads, likely related to metabolic or medication effects.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 11.8 x10^9/L (H), Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 34.1 % (L), Neutrophils 78 % (H), Lymphocytes 12 % (L), Glucose 142 mg/dL (H), AST 42 U/L (H), Albumin 3.4 g/dL (L), TSH 6.8 mIU/L (H), CRP 18.4 mg/L (H), Prolactin 42 ng/mL (H), Levetiracetam level 8.2 mcg/mL (L).

background.continuity: Continuous with intermittent left temporal polymorphic delta activity
background.symmetry: Persistent amplitude asymmetry with attenuation over left temporal region
background.sleep_features: Sleep architecture preserved; interictal discharges more frequent during NREM sleep

findings.location: Left anterior temporal (F7-T3), maximum at T3
findings.frequency: 2-5 per hour during wakefulness, 8-12 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Biphasic sharp waves with aftergoing slow wave, amplitude 80-120 microvolts, duration 70-120 ms
findings.state: Awake and sleep, more frequent in sleep

findings.location: Left temporal onset (T3-T5), with secondary spread to left frontal and then bilateral
findings.frequency: Three electrographic seizures captured over 72 hours of monitoring
findings.morphology: Seizure onset: rhythmic 5-7 Hz theta evolving to 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta with superimposed fast activity. Duration 45-90 seconds. Post-ictal regional left temporal attenuation lasting 2-5 minutes.
findings.state: Two during wakefulness, one during sleep

findings.type: Intermittent left temporal polymorphic delta activity
findings.frequency: Continuous during post-ictal periods, intermittent interictally

output.impression: Markedly abnormal prolonged video-EEG monitoring. Three electrographic seizures captured with left temporal onset, all with clinical correlate of focal impaired awareness seizures with automatisms. Interictal left anterior temporal sharp waves. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.type: Enhancing metastasis with mesial temporal involvement
findings.location: Left temporal lobe with extension into the left hippocampal body and amygdala

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense; perilesional edema clearly involves the hippocampal formation
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal_thin_cuts: Abnormal signal within the left hippocampal body with loss of normal internal architecture
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Ring enhancement; the enhancing margin directly abuts the left hippocampal body

findings.mass_effect: Effacement of left temporal horn. Mild compression of left hippocampal formation.
findings.borders: Irregular, infiltrative margin along the hippocampal interface

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: Cannot be reliably measured due to direct lesion involvement
volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 3.4 cm3 (45th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.note: Left hippocampal volumetrics invalid due to tumor infiltration

additional_observations: Thin coronal T2 and FLAIR sequences confirm tumor extension into left mesial temporal structures
No cortical dysplasia or other epileptogenic lesion identified beyond the metastasis
output.impression: The lesion's anatomic relationship to mesial temporal structures provides a clear epileptogenic substrate for the patient's focal impaired awareness seizures with temporal semiology. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy seizures brain metastasis focal epilepsy

results.title: Seizures and brain metastases in patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors: a systematic review
results.summary: Brain metastases are an independent risk factor for seizures in cancer patients, with an incidence of 20-35% depending on tumor type and location. Temporal lobe metastases carry particularly high seizure risk. Immune checkpoint inhibitors may increase perilesional edema, potentially lowering seizure threshold.

results.title: Neurological immune-related adverse events from checkpoint inhibitors: distinguishing encephalitis from seizures
results.summary: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated neurological toxicity includes encephalitis, neuropathy, myasthenia gravis, and seizures. Seizures may occur as an isolated phenomenon related to brain metastasis irritation rather than immune-mediated encephalitis. The distinction is important as management differs: seizures from structural lesions require AEDs and lesion management, while immune encephalitis requires immunosuppression.

results.title: Temporal lobe epilepsy secondary to brain metastases: outcomes with surgical and medical management
results.summary: Tumor-related epilepsy from temporal lobe metastases often presents with focal impaired awareness seizures with semiology indistinguishable from mesial temporal sclerosis-related epilepsy. Seizure control is best achieved through a combined approach of AED optimization and definitive lesion treatment (surgery or radiosurgery).

output.summary: Literature supports that temporal lobe brain metastases are a well-recognized cause of focal epilepsy, with seizure incidence of 20-35%. The semiology of metastasis-related temporal lobe seizures is identical to other forms of temporal lobe epilepsy. Checkpoint inhibitors may exacerbate seizure risk through increased perilesional inflammation. Management requires both AED optimization and definitive lesion treatment. It is important to distinguish seizures from immune-mediated encephalitis, as management differs significantly.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam dose optimization in patient on ipilimumab/nivolumab and dexamethasone
interactions: Dexamethasone may induce hepatic enzymes (primarily CYP3A4), but levetiracetam is minimally hepatically metabolized (primarily renal elimination), so this interaction is not clinically significant for levetiracetam
Levetiracetam has no known significant pharmacokinetic interactions with checkpoint inhibitors (ipilimumab, nivolumab)
Dexamethasone at current doses may reduce the efficacy of checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy by suppressing immune activation
warnings: Levetiracetam can cause irritability, mood changes, and fatigue — which may overlap with immunotherapy side effects and complicate symptom attribution
Subtherapeutic levetiracetam levels increase seizure risk, especially with a known structural epileptogenic lesion
If considering lacosamide as add-on therapy: generally safe with immunotherapy, primarily hepatically metabolized via CYP2C19
alternatives: Lacosamide 50-100 mg BID as add-on (good tolerability, IV formulation available)
Brivaracetam 25-50 mg BID (similar mechanism to levetiracetam with possibly fewer behavioral side effects)
Avoid enzyme-inducing AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) as they may interact with oncologic therapies

findings.signal: FDG_uptake: Markedly increased FDG uptake (SUVmax 12.4) within the enhancing component of the left temporal metastasis, consistent with metabolically active tumor; perilesional: Ring of hypometabolism surrounding the hypermetabolic core, extending into the left hippocampal region and left temporal neocortex; contralateral: Normal FDG uptake in the right temporal lobe for comparison

output.impression: The metabolic pattern supports true tumor progression rather than pseudoprogression and delineates the epileptogenic zone extending beyond the enhancing lesion margin. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy — temporal lobe, secondary to left temporal lobe brain metastasis (melanoma)

differential.diagnosis: Focal epilepsy secondary to left temporal metastasis
differential.key_evidence: Stereotyped episodes with temporal semiology (epigastric aura, deja vu, behavioral arrest, oroalimentary automatisms), growing left temporal lesion with hippocampal involvement, subtherapeutic AED levels, elevated post-ictal prolactin
Temporal relationship with immunotherapy is suggestive, but stereotyped semiology, structural lesion, negative autoimmune panels, and absence of progressive encephalopathy argue against

differential.diagnosis: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated motor neuropathy (concurrent, not explaining episodes)
differential.key_evidence: Mild motor axonal neuropathy on EMG/NCS, temporal relationship with immunotherapy, lower extremity weakness; however, this does not explain the paroxysmal episodes
Negative paraneoplastic antibody panel, no progressive cognitive decline, stereotyped rather than progressive symptoms
Normal ECG, episodes have positive motor phenomena (automatisms) and post-ictal confusion inconsistent with syncope

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Non-specific left temporal slowing with benign variants; no definitive epileptiform discharges on initial routine study

optimal_actions.action: Review brain MRI to assess temporal lesion and mass effect
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Growth of left temporal metastasis with perilesional edema involving hippocampal formation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including AED levels, metabolic panel, and thyroid function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level, elevated TSH, elevated post-ictal prolactin, steroid-related metabolic derangements

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac etiology for episodes of unresponsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with non-specific T-wave changes; no arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal seizures captured with clinical correlation confirming focal epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Request 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol to evaluate hippocampal involvement
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmation of tumor extension into hippocampal body and amygdala

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF analysis to evaluate for autoimmune or paraneoplastic etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild pleocytosis with elevated protein; negative autoimmune and paraneoplastic panels

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for AED optimization in the setting of immunotherapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam safe with immunotherapy; avoid enzyme-inducing AEDs

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on seizure management in brain metastasis patients on checkpoint inhibitors
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting AED optimization and definitive lesion treatment for seizure control

critical_actions: Obtain prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture and characterize the paroxysmal episodes
Review brain MRI to assess the left temporal metastasis and its relationship to mesial temporal structures
Check levetiracetam serum level and optimize antiepileptic drug therapy
Distinguish focal seizures from immune-mediated encephalitis, as management differs significantly
Ensure adequate seizure precautions given breakthrough events
Coordinate with oncology regarding definitive management of the growing temporal metastasis
contraindicated_actions: Attributing paroxysmal episodes to anxiety or chemotherapy side effects without diagnostic workup
Discontinuing levetiracetam without adequate seizure evaluation
Starting enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, carbamazepine) that may interfere with oncologic therapies
Initiating high-dose corticosteroids for presumed immune encephalitis without confirming the diagnosis, as steroids may mask symptoms and affect immunotherapy efficacy
Withholding EEG monitoring due to assumption that the known metastasis fully explains all symptoms
key_reasoning_points: The stereotyped semiology (epigastric aura, deja vu, behavioral arrest, oroalimentary automatisms, right hand automatisms, head version to right) is classic for left temporal lobe seizures, not panic attacks or chemotherapy side effects
A growing left temporal metastasis with perilesional edema extending into the hippocampal formation provides a clear structural epileptogenic substrate
Subtherapeutic levetiracetam levels (8.2 mcg/mL, therapeutic range 12-46) due to non-adherence explain breakthrough seizures despite prophylaxis
Elevated post-ictal prolactin (42 ng/mL, drawn 30 minutes after event) supports an epileptic mechanism over non-epileptic events
The initial routine EEG was non-diagnostic (as expected — single routine EEG sensitivity is only 29-55%), requiring prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture seizures
The concurrent mild motor axonal neuropathy (likely checkpoint inhibitor-related) is a separate process and should not distract from the primary diagnosis of focal epilepsy
Negative autoimmune encephalitis and paraneoplastic panels argue against immune-mediated encephalitis, confirming the seizures are structural rather than autoimmune in etiology

The diagnostic puzzle arises from multiple competing hypotheses: progressive metastatic disease, immune-mediated neurotoxicity (encephalitis vs neuropathy), paraneoplastic syndrome, and structural epilepsy — requiring systematic evaluation to distinguish

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: paroxysmal episodes in a patient with metastatic melanoma on immunotherapy are initially attributed to treatment side effects and anxiety. Multiple competing diagnostic hypotheses (tumor progression, immune encephalitis, paraneoplastic syndrome, checkpoint inhibitor neuropathy) obscure the primary diagnosis of focal temporal lobe epilepsy. Initial routine EEG is non-diagnostic, key findings are subtherapeutic AED levels and elevated post-ictal prolactin, and definitive diagnosis requires prolonged video-EEG monitoring.
